html:
IV

CarlтАЩs adviser had been less efficient than HughтАЩs; therefore he knew what his courses were, where the classes met and the hours, the names of his instructors, and the requirements other than Latin for a B.S. degree. Carl said that he was taking a B.S. because he had had a year of Greek at Kane and was therefore perfectly competent to make full use of the language; he could read the letters on the front doors of the fraternity houses.

The boys found that their courses were the same but that they were in different sections. Hugh was in a dilemma; he could make nothing out of his card.

тАЬHere,тАЭ said Carl, тАЬgive the thing to me. My adviser was a good scout and wised me up. This P.C. isnтАЩt paper cutting as you might suppose; itтАЩs gym. YouтАЩll get out of that by signing up for track. P.C. means physical culture. Think of that! You can sign up for track any time tomorrow down at the gym. And E I, 7 means that youтАЩre in English I, Section 7; and M is math. You re in Section 3. Lat means Latin, of courseтБатАФSection 6. My adviserтБатАФhe tried pretty hard to be funnyтБатАФsaid that G.S. wasnтАЩt glorious salvation but general science. That meets in the big lecture hall in Cranston. We all go to that. And H I, 4 means that you are in Section 4 of History I. See? ThatтАЩs all there is to it. Now this thingтАЭтБатАФhe held up a printed scheduleтБатАФтАЬtells you where the classes meet.тАЭ

With a great deal of labor, discussion, and profanity they finally got a schedule made out that meant something to Hugh. He heaved a Brobdingnagian sigh of relief when they finished.

тАЬWell,тАЭ he exclaimed, тАЬthatтАЩs that! At last I know where IтАЩm going. You certainly saved my life. I know where all the buildings are; so it ought to be easy.тАЭ

тАЬSure,тАЭ said Carl encouragingly; тАЬitтАЩs easy. Now thereтАЩs nothing to do till tomorrow until eight forty-five when we attend chapel to the glory of the Lord. I think IтАЩll pray tomorrow; I may need it. Christ! I hate to study.тАЭ

тАЬMe, too,тАЭ Hugh lied. He really loved books, but somehow he couldnтАЩt admit the fact, which had suddenly become shameful, to Carl. тАЬLetтАЩs go to the movies,тАЭ he suggested, changing the subject for safety.

тАЬRight-o!тАЭ Carl put on his freshman cap and flung HughтАЩs to him. тАЬGloria Nielsen is there, and sheтАЩs a pash baby. Ought to be a good fillum.тАЭ

The Blue and OrangeтБатАФit was the only movie theater in townтБатАФwas almost full when the boys arrived. Only a few seats near the front were still vacant. A freshman started down the aisle, his тАЬbaby bonnetтАЭ stuck jauntily on the back of his head.

тАЬFreshman!тАЭтБатАКтБатАж тАЬKill him!тАЭтБатАКтБатАж тАЬMurder the frosh!тАЭ Shouts came from all parts of the house, and an instant later hundreds of peanuts shot swiftly at the startled freshman. тАЬCap! Cap! Cap off!тАЭ There was a panic of excitement. Upper-classmen were standing on their chairs to get free throwing room. The freshman snatched off his cap, drew his head like a scared turtle down into his coat collar, and ran for a seat. Hugh and Carl tucked their caps into their coat pockets and attempted to stroll nonchalantly down the aisle. They hadnтАЩt taken three steps before the bombardment began. Like their classmate, they ran for safety.

Then someone in the front of the theatre threw a peanut at someone in the rear. The fight was on! Yelling like madmen, the students stood on their chairs and hurled peanuts, the front and rear of the house automatically dividing into enemy camps. When the fight was at its hottest, three girls entered.

тАЬWimmen! Wimmen!тАЭ As the girls walked down the aisle, infinitely pleased with their reception, five hundred men stamped in time with their steps.

No sooner were the girls seated than there was a scramble in one corner, an excited scuffling of feet. тАЬIтАЩve got it!тАЭ a boy screamed. He stood on his chair and held up a live mouse by its tail. There was a shout of applause and thenтБатАФтАЬPlay catch!тАЭ

The boy dropped the writhing mouse into a peanut bag, screwed the open end tight-closed, and then threw the bag far across the room. Another boy caught it and threw it, this time over the girlsтАЩ heads. They screamed and jumped upon their chairs, holding their skirts, and dancing up and down in assumed terror. Back over their heads, back and over, again and again the bagged mouse was thrown while the girls screamed and the boys roared with delight. Suddenly one of the girls threw up her arm, caught the bag deftly, held it for a second, and then tossed it into the rear of the theater.

Cheers of terrifying violence broke loose: тАЬRay! Ray! Atta girl! Hot dog! Ray, ray!тАЭ And then the lights went out.

тАЬMoosick! Moosick! Moo-sick!тАЭ The audience stamped and roared, whistled and howled. тАЬMoosick! We want moosick!тАЭ

The pianist, an undergraduate, calmly strolled down the aisle.

тАЬGet a move on!тАЭтБатАКтБатАж тАЬEarn your salary!тАЭтБатАКтБатАж тАЬGive us moosick!тАЭ

The pianist paused to thumb his nose casually at the entire audience, and then amid shouts and hisses sat down at the piano and began to play тАЬLove Nest.тАЭ

Immediately the boys began to whistle, and as the comedy was utterly stupid, they relieved their boredom by whistling the various tunes that the pianist played until the miserable film flickered out.

Then the тАЬfeatureтАЭ and the fun began. During the stretches of pure narrative, the boys whistled, but when there was any real action they talked. The picture was a melodrama of тАЬlove and hate,тАЭ as the advertisement said.

The boys told the actors what to do; they revealed to them the secrets of the plot. тАЬSheтАЩs hiding behind the door, Harold. No, no! Not that way. Hey, dumbbellтБатАФbehind the door.тАЭтБатАКтБатАж тАЬCatch him, Gloria; heтАЩs only shy!тАЭтБатАКтБатАж тАЬNo, thatтАЩs not him!тАЭ

The climactic fight brought shouts of encouragementтБатАФto the villain. тАЬKill him!тАЭтБатАКтБатАж тАЬShoot one to his kidneys!тАЭтБатАКтБатАж тАЬAhhhhh,тАЭ as the villain hit the hero in the stomach.тБатАКтБатАж тАЬMuss his hair. Attaboy!тАЭтБатАКтБатАж тАЬKill the skunk!тАЭ And finally groans of despair when the hero won his inevitable victory.

But it was the love scenes that aroused the greatest ardor and joy. The hero was given careful instructions. тАЬSome neckinтАЩ, Harold!тАЭтБатАКтБатАж тАЬKiss her! Kiss her! Ahhh!тАЭтБатАКтБатАж тАЬHarold, Harold, youтАЩre getting rough!тАЭтБатАКтБатАж тАЬSheтАЩs vamping you, Harold!тАЭтБатАКтБатАж тАЬStop it; Gloria; heтАЩs a good boy.тАЭ And so on until the picture ended in the usual closeup of the hero and heroine silhouetted in a tender embrace against the setting sun. The boys breathed тАЬAhhhhтАЭ and тАЬOooohтАЭ ecstaticallyтБатАФand laughed. The meretricious melodrama did not fool them, but they delighted in its absurdities.

The lights flashed on and the crowd filed out, тАЬwisecrackingтАЭ about the picture and commenting favorably on the heroineтАЩs figure. There were shouts to this fellow or that fellow to come on over and play bridge, and suggestions here and there to go to a drug store and get a drink.

Hugh and Carl strolled home over the dark campus, both of them radiant with excitement, Hugh frankly so.

тАЬGolly, I did enjoy that,тАЭ he exclaimed. тАЬI never had a better time. It was sure hot stuff. I donтАЩt want to go to the room; letтАЩs walk for a while.тАЭ

тАЬYeah, it was pretty good,тАЭ Carl admitted. тАЬNope, I canтАЩt go walking; gotta write a letter.тАЭ

тАЬWho to? The harem?тАЭ

Carl hunched his shoulders until his ears touched his coat collar. тАЬGettinтАЩ cold. FallтАЩs here. Nope, not the harem. My old lady.тАЭ

Hugh looked at him bewildered. He was finding Carl more and more a conundrum. He consistently called his mother his old lady, insisted that she was a damned nuisanceтБатАФand wrote to her every night. Hugh was writing to his mother only twice a week. It was very confusing.тБатАКтБатАж
